segments.s084-g97: چنین is an adverb meaning “thus/so” placed at the start to report speech. گفت is the past 3rd‑person verb “he said” (literary word order here: adverb + verb + agent). با marks the agent (“by/with”) in this poetic register, and نامور (an adjective “renowned”) precedes نامجوی, a proper name or epithet; together they mean “the renowned Namjuy.”
segments.s084-g98: که is the subordinating particle “that” introducing the content of the speech (a reported clause). من is the explicit subject “I.” خون is the direct object “blood.” به کین is a prepositional phrase “for/in vengeance” that qualifies the action. اندر آرم is the verb phrase: اندر (archaic) “into/in” + آرم (1st‑person form) meaning “I put/bring / I will put” (poetic present/future sense), and the final به جوی gives the location “into the stream.” The line literally stacks prepositional elements (خون به کین اندر آرم به جوی) = “I put blood, for vengeance, into the stream.”
segments.s084-g73: یکی = 'one' (indefinite/one of them). نره شیرست is a condensed predicate where شیرست = شیر است (archaic/poetic contraction of است): 'is a male lion' (نره qualifies شیر). روز شکار is a temporal adjunct 'on the day of the hunt' placed after the predicate to mark the occasion.
segments.s084-g74: یکی = 'one (another)'. پیل جنگی = 'an elephant (پیل) qualified as warlike/for war (جنگی)'. گه کارزار = 'at the time/occasion (گه) of battle (کارزار)' — گه is a temporal noun 'occasion/time' and follows جنگی to indicate when that elephant shows its nature.
segments.s084-g75: ترا is written as the clitic sequence ت + را (ت = 2nd‑person object, را = object marker), meaning 'you' as the thing affected. نیز 'also'. با او 'with him'. بباید شدن is a periphrastic necessity: بباید (must/ought) + شدن (here functioning as copula/infinitive 'become/stand') => 'you must be (with him)'.
segments.s084-g76: به هر = 'in/for every'. بیش و کم is a fixed binary expression 'more and less' meaning 'great and small' (i.e. all matters). رای فرخ زدن: رای 'opinion/plan/decision' modified by فرخ 'fortunate'; زدن with رای is an idiom meaning 'to give/express an opinion or vote' (here 'to cast a fortunate/favourable opinion'). The whole phrase states the action you should perform in all matters.
segments.s084-g77: نبیره 'scion/descendant' as subject; که introduces a relative clause 'who'. کین نیا را نجست = object كین (vengeance) marked by را with نیا 'ancestor' as possessor/target: 'sought vengeance against (or for) the ancestor'; نجست is past of جستن 'to seek' (here 'sought').
segments.s084-g78: سزد‌گر functions as 'fitting/appropriate' (classical/poetic form). نخوانی is a negative present/optative second‑person form of خواندن here meaning 'you do not call/you should not call'. نژادش 'his lineage' (نژاد + possessive -ش). درست 'correct/true' is the predicate modifier: 'it is fitting that you do not call his lineage correct.'
segments.s084-g79: چو = 'when'. از دامن ابر چین = a prepositional phrase 'from the hem (دامن) of the cloud (ابر) of folds (چین)' — چین here 'fold' describing the cloud. کم شود = 'becomes little/diminished' (کم + شود), i.e. the cloud's hem thins or is reduced; this is the temporal subordinate clause.
segments.s084-g80: بیابان 'plain/wilderness'. ز باران 'from/because of the rain' (ز is the poetic form of از). پر از نم شود = 'becomes full of moisture' (پر = full, از = of, نم = moisture/dew; شود = becomes). The word order places the source (rain) and the result (full of moisture) after the subject.
segments.s084-g9: ز is the poetic preposition 'about / concerning' that heads this list. کار منوچهر is a genitive construction (کارِ منوچهر) meaning 'Manuchehr's deeds/affairs' (noun + possessor); از لشکرش repeats the preposition with the possessive suffix -ش 'his' on لشکر ('his army'), so the two phrases are coordinated as items governed by ز.
segments.s084-g10: This continues the enumerative pattern. ز again marks 'from/about' (here listing objects), followed by گردان 'troops/divisions' and سالار 'commanders' joined by و. سپس از کشورش uses از + کشور + possessive -ش 'his realm'; the prepositions and و set up a paratactic list rather than a full clause.
segments.s084-g11: همه is a universal quantifier 'all' modifying the plural noun نامداران 'renowned ones' (nominalized adjective). کشورش is the possessed noun 'his country/realm' (کشور + possessive -ش). را is the definite object marker showing this whole phrase is a definite direct object of the verb that follows.
segments.s084-g12: بخواند is a third-person singular narrative verb here meaning 'he summoned/called' (in epic Persian the simple past/narrative form often appears as بخواند). It governs both the previous object (همه نامداران کشورش را) and the coordinated object following the conjunction و. بزرگان لشکرش را is another definite object (بزرگان = 'great ones', لشکرش = 'his army'), with را marking it.
segments.s084-g13: چو is the comparative/simile particle 'like / as', here introducing a list of proper names (ارجسپ، گرسیوز، بارمان). The conjunction و simply links the names; there is no separate verb in this fragment—the phrase is a simile that modifies or compares to something in the surrounding context.
segments.s084-g14: Again چو begins a simile 'like Kalbad'. جنگی is an adjectival epithet 'warlike/warrior' modifying the following name phrase هژبر دمان; here the adjective precedes the name as an epithet. هژبر دمان functions as a two-part proper name/epithet (name + second element).
segments.s084-g15: سپهبدش is سپهبد + possessive -ش 'his spahbed/commander-in-chief'. چون (variant of چو) introduces a comparison 'like', followed by the personal name ویسه and the epithet/compound تیزچنگ. The phrase sets up a likeness between the spahbed and that named figure.
segments.s084-g16: که is the relative particle 'who/that' introducing a relative clause describing the named person. سالار is 'commander/chief'. بد here is an archaic form representing the past copula ('was' = بود) in this epic style (none of the listed senses correspond to that verbal use, so mark as -1). بر + سپاه means 'over/on the army' (بر = over/against), and پشنگ is a proper name (the army/person named Pashang). Together: 'who was commander over Pashang's host.'
segments.s084-g49: سپهبد (spahbed) is the sentence subject; چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; شایسته is an adjective modifying پسر. بیند is the present/subjunctive-form of دیدن placed after the adjective phrase (verb‑final ordering is common in poetic Persian), so the clause reads literally 'the spahbed when a worthy son sees.'
segments.s084-g50: سزد‌گر is an agentive/adjectival word ‘the fitting one / one who deems fit’ and functions here as the patient/recipient description; برآرد is a present/optative verb meaning 'brings up/sets up' or 'raises'; به + خورشید سر is a prepositional phrase 'to the sun’s head' (خورشید = sun; سر = head/chief), indicating the elevated position to which the fitting person is raised. Word order places the verb before its prepositional complement, as is normal in Ferdowsi's diction.
segments.s084-g51: پس از مرگ is the temporal phrase 'after the death'; باشد here has an optative/prescriptive force 'should be / shall be' rather than a plain imperative; سر او به جای literally 'his head in place' uses سر = 'post/position' and به جای = 'in place / filled', so the clause means 'after his death his position should be filled.'
segments.s084-g52: ازیرا پسر is an appositive: the proper name Azira followed by the identifier 'son'; نام زد is a simple past construction 'named / gave the name' (زد here = 'utter/sound' in a light compound), and رهنمای is the role given ('guide' / 'advisor'), so the clause says 'he named Azira, the son, (as) guide.'
segments.s084-g53: چو is 'when'; شد + ساخته forms a passive/perfect sense 'was made / had been made' (شد functioning as a verbal auxiliary here); کار جنگ آزمای is a noun phrase 'the work/affair of war, battle‑tested' (جنگ modifying کار and آزمای as an adjectival element), so the whole clause reads 'when the war-work was made' i.e. 'when the war preparations were ready.'
segments.s084-g54: به کاخ آمد is a locative prepositional phrase plus past verb 'came to the palace'; اغریرث is a proper name presented after the verb (common in older Persian narrative order), and رهنمای immediately qualifies him as 'the guide' (appositive). The clause therefore means 'Aghrirath the guide came to the palace.'
segments.s084-g55: به پیش پدر is the prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before the father'; شد is 'became / came to be' used here as a simple past/state verb; پراندیشه دل is a compound adjective+noun 'with a heart full of thought' (پراندیشه = pensive / full of thought; دل = heart), so the clause reads 'he came before his father, pensive.'
segments.s084-g56: که introduces a subordinate (here causal/explanatory) clause 'for / because'; اندیشه دارد is present-tense 'has thought' (دارد = 'possesses / holds'); همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle 'still / continually'; پیشه دل is a metaphorical genitive 'the heart's preoccupation / concern' (پیشه used as 'occupation/concern'). Together the clause explains the reason: 'for his heart still carried that concern.'
segments.s084-g89: منوچهر is the proper-name subject; از آن جایگه is a prepositional phrase (‘from that place’) giving origin or location; جنگجوی is a predicative or appositional noun (‘(as) a warrior’) placed after the location. Word order (Name + prepositional origin + role) is typical of epic Persian apposition.
segments.s084-g90: به کینه is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘with hatred’ (به + abstract noun); سوی تور is another prepositional phrase ‘toward Tūr’ (proper name). بنهاد روی is a classical verbal expression: روی بنهادن = ‘to set/turn one’s face (toward)’, so بنهاد is the past action ‘he turned/set’ and روی is the complement ‘face’. The verb follows the directional phrases, as is normal in this register.
segments.s084-g91: بکوشید is a past narrative form ‘he strove/attempted’ (from کوشیدن). با قارن is the accompaniment phrase ‘with Qāren’ (a named companion). رزم زن is a compound in which زن functions as a verbal notion ‘striking/fighting’ — together ‘rāzm-zan’ = ‘one who fights’ or ‘waging battle’ (so the sequence means ‘he strove, fighting in battle with Qāren’).
segments.s084-g92: دگر here means ‘again / meanwhile / another’; گرد is used as a verb/adverbial ‘to go about / move around’; گرشاسپ is the proper-name subject (Garshasp); زان انجمن = ز + آن + انجمن is a fused prepositional phrase ‘from/around that assembly’ (زان = ‘from that/therefore’ + انجمن = ‘assembly’). Together: ‘Garshasp again went about that assembly.’
segments.s084-g93: مگر here introduces a rhetorical/conditional sense (‘could it be that…/perhaps…’); دست یابید is the verbal phrase ‘reach/lay hands on’ (دست = grasp/control; یابید = attain/achieve, past/narrative form). بر دشت کین is the goal phrase ‘on/upon the plain of vengeance’ (بر = on/upon; دشت = plain; کین = vengeance).
segments.s084-g94: برین is the prepositional + demonstrative fusion (بر + این = ‘upon/against these’); دو سرافراز ایران‌زمین is the noun phrase ‘the two proud/lofty ones of the Iranian land’ (دو = two; سرافراز = proud/lofty/honored; ایران‌زمین = land of Iran). The whole phrase specifies the targets of the vengeance.
segments.s084-g95: روان نیاگان ما is a possessive construction ‘the souls of our ancestors’ (روان = souls; نیاگان = ancestors; ما = our). خوش کنید is a jussive/optative or imperative‑style verb form ‘make glad / cause to be happy’ (کنید used as a request/command in epic diction).
segments.s084-g96: دل بدسگالان is ‘the hearts of the ill‑omened/ill‑fated’ (دل = heart; بدسگالان = plural ‘the ill‑omened’). پرآتش کنید is a causative phrase ‘make (them) full of fire / set (them) aflame’ (پرآتش = fiery/full‑of‑fire; کنید = cause/make). The verb mirrors the previous clause, forming a pair of imperative wishes.
segments.s084-g17: A nominal phrase identifying the man: an epithet + personal name + patronymic. Word order is title (جهان‌پهلوان) + given name (پورش) + father's name (افراسیاب), i.e. “the world‑champion Poursh, (of) Afrasiab.” This group functions as the person referred to by the following verb.
segments.s084-g18: بخواندش is a past tense verb with the enclitic object -ش (him/it); here it is best read as “(he) summoned him/called him.” درنگی is adverbial/noun used adverbially “a short pause/hesitation.” و connects the clauses “and,” آمد is past tense “(he) came,” and شتاب is a noun used adverbially “with haste” (i.e. “came hastily”).
segments.s084-g19: سخن راند is a poetic collocation: سخن (“speech/words”) + راند (past of راندن). Here راند is used idiomatically to mean “uttered/expressed” (i.e. ‘he spoke’). از ... گفت is the common construction meaning “spoke of / said about …” with از marking the topic (of Tūr and of Salm). و simply coordinates the two names.
segments.s084-g20: که introduces an indirect statement or reported content (“saying that …”). کین means “vengeance/hatred.” زیر دامن is a prepositional phrase literally “under the hem/skirt” used figuratively for ‘secretly/concealed.’ نشاید is an impersonal/fixed present form meaning “it is not fitting / it ought not to,” and نهفت is a past participle/adjective meaning “hidden/concealed.” Together: “that such vengeance should not be kept hidden under the hem.”
segments.s084-g21: A rhetorical question formed by کسی را کجا + clause: کسی را (kasi + rā) places emphasis/indefinite focus on ‘anyone,’ کجا is the interrogative ‘where/anywhere’ used idiomatically (‘is there anyone’), and مغز جوشیده نیست is a negated predicate: مغز (brain/mind) + جوشیده (seething/boiling, i.e. excited) + نیست (is not). Literally: “Is there anyone whose mind is not boiling?”
segments.s084-g22: برو here is an imperative/particle used to introduce a conclusion or admonition (“go/consider/observe” roughly = “mark this”); بر چنین کار is a prepositional phrase meaning “with respect to such a deed” (بر = on/about, چنین کار = such a deed). پوشیده نیست is a predicate: پوشیده (hidden, participle/adjective) + نیست (is not). Together: “Consider: for such a deed there is no hiding.”
segments.s084-g23: که introduces a clause explaining content (“that …”). با ما means “to/against us” or “with regard to us,” چه کردند is an embedded question/relative clause meaning “what they did,” and ایرانیان is the agent “the Iranians.” The phrase frames the following as the report of Iranian actions toward the speaker’s side.
segments.s084-g24: بدی را is the direct object “the evil/wrong” with the object marker را. ببستند is past tense plural (they) from بستن used here in the sense “bound/tied up / carried out (wrapping up wrongs)” — idiomatically “they committed/inflicted” or “they tied up the evils.” یک‌یک is an adverbial ‘one by one,’ and میان here means ‘among / in succession’ (i.e. one after another). The clause therefore says the Iranians enacted evils one by one.
segments.s084-g57: چنین گفت is a formulaic opening: چنین (thus) + گفت (said). کای is a vocative particle ("O"); it addresses someone directly. کار دیده is an adjective phrase where دیده is a past-participial/ adjectival use meaning "seen/experienced," modifying کار (affair/deed) so together implying "experienced in affairs; a veteran." پدر is the noun 'father' and follows that descriptive phrase ("son of an experienced father" / "whose father is experienced").
segments.s084-g58: ز ترکان indicates origin/party (ز = 'from' or 'among' in poetic register) and ترکان is the group 'the Turks.' به مردی here introduces the indefinite agent/individual ('a man' — مردی) and the preposition به plays a light locative/associative role ('in/among' or 'by/there has arisen in the person of a man'). برآورده is a past participle/compound verbal adjective meaning 'has risen/lifted up' (here used predicatively of سر), and سر ('head') is the thing lifted — an idiom meaning 'has shown himself/raised his head, become prominent.'
segments.s084-g59: منوچهر is the proper name subject. از ایران ('from Iran' / 'in Iran') uses از to mark provenance or domain (here 'in/from among the Iranians'). اگر is the conditional 'if.' کم here is adjectival/nominal meaning 'few / diminished'; it describes quantity or strength. شدست is an older/poetic past/perfect form (شد + -ست) meaning 'has become' (it functions as the finite verb of the clause).
segments.s084-g60: سپهدار is the title/noun 'commander' and begins the main clause. چون here means 'like' or 'as' and introduces the comparison to سام (the proper name Sam). نیرم is an adjective meaning 'soft/weak' (archaic/toepic usage), and شدست again is the same poetic past 'has become' — so the clause says the commander 'has become weak like Sam.'
segments.s084-g61: چو here functions as 'like/as' and introduces examples: گرشاسپ is a proper name (a famed hero). و simply coordinates. the second چون again 'like' before قارن; رزم زن is a compound where رزم (battle) + زن (literally 'striker'/'one who strikes') together mean 'warrior' or 'battle-fighter.' The phrase names two exemplary fighters.
segments.s084-g62: جز این نامداران آن انجمن is a concise negative/exclusive comment: جز = 'except' or 'apart from'; این نامداران = 'these renowned/famed men' (نامداران is plural of نامدار 'renowned'); آن انجمن = 'that assembly/company.' The clause implies that, aside from the named warriors, the assembly has no other notable men.
segments.s084-g63: تو دانی که = 'Do you know that / do you know whether' (تو = you, دانی = you know in present; که introduces the subordinate content clause). با + سلم و تور names the victims/participants: here سلم and تور are proper names (sons of Fereydun). سترگ is an epithet meaning 'great/mighty' applied to the pair; in older Persian it functions as an adjective after the coordinated names.
segments.s084-g64: چه آمد is a standard interrogative phrase 'what came/happened' (چه = what; آمد = came/happened). ازان = 'from that' (archaic contraction of از + آن). تیغ‌زن is a compound 'sword-striker' = 'swordsman' or 'one who strikes with a blade.' پیر is 'old' and here qualifies گرگ 'wolf' (a figurative/possibly literal enemy), so تیغ‌زن پیر گرگ = 'that old, sword-wielding wolf.' The clause asks what happened to Salm and Tur at that individual's hands.
segments.s084-g41: مغز پشنگ is a genitive phrase 'Pashang's mind/inner thought'; the verb آمد (came) follows, and شتاب (haste) is the noun that 'came into' his mind. The preposition به + مغز + اندر آمد is a verbal phrase meaning 'entered into (his) mind'—the copular idea 'he became hasty' is expressed by this intransitive construction.
segments.s084-g42: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; دید is a simple past verb 'he saw' with the subject omitted (referring back to Pashang). آن سهی قد is a noun phrase 'that dark (سهی) stature (قد)' modifying the name افراسیاب, so the clause reads 'when he saw that dark, tall Afrasiab.'
segments.s084-g43: This is a nominal descriptive string listing attributes: بازوی شیر 'the arm (like) of a lion' and هم زور پیل 'also the strength/might of an elephant.' There is no finite verb inside this segment; it functions as an adjectival description of the figure seen.
segments.s084-g44: وزو here functions idiomatically as 'from him' (i.e., 'and from him'); سایه گسترده is a predicative phrase 'a shadow spread' and بر چند میل means 'over several miles'—بر used in the sense 'over/covering'. The whole segment says that his shadow extended for several miles.
segments.s084-g45: زبانش is the possessed noun 'his tongue'; به کردار is an adverbial phrase 'in (its) manner' and modifies the following predicate. برنده تیغ is a simile/metaphor 'a cutting sword'—the copula is omitted (typical of classical Persian), so the clause means 'his tongue was like a cutting sword.'
segments.s084-g46: چو introduces simile 'like/as'; دریا دل is an inverted simile 'heart like the sea' (i.e., very large/ generous), and the conjunction و links the next simile: کف چو بارنده میغ 'palm/hand like a rain‑bearing blade(s),' i.e., his blows fell like a rain of blades. Again the copula is omitted and comparison is made by چو.
segments.s084-g47: بفرمود is an honorific past verb 'he ordered/commanded' and introduces a subordinate clause with تا + present/subjunctive. تا + برکشد yields the meaning 'that (someone) draw/raise' (here, draw the sword). تیغ جنگ is a genitive 'the sword of war' and is the object of برکشد.
segments.s084-g48: This continues the commanded result: به ایران indicates the direction 'to/into Iran' and شود is the verb of the subordinate clause (here meaning roughly 'proceed/go' or 'come to be in'); با سپاه پشنگ is an instrumental/adverbial phrase 'with Pashang's army.' The clause is tied to the preceding بفرمود تا, so the whole means 'he ordered that they go to Iran with Pashang's host.'
segments.s084-g65: This is a noun phrase introducing a person by lineage and title. نیا here means 'ancestor' and combines with the following item زادشم (treated here as a name/epithet) to give an ancestral/lineage tag; شاه توران سپاه is a cluster in which شاه = 'king' is modified by توران ('Turan') and سپاه ('host/army') — the order places the title before its territorial/collective modifiers, yielding 'the king (leader) of Turan's army'.
segments.s084-g66: که introduces a relative clause describing the preceding head ('whose ...'). ترگش is ترگ + ـش (possessive 'his'), here 'his rag/banner/standard' (poetic use). همی is the older habitual/continuous marker 'ever/always'. سود is a nominal/verb-like item 'benefit' used predicatively with the preposition بر governing چرخ و ماه ('upon heaven and moon'). The clause thus means roughly 'whose standard ever stretches/has effect upon the sky and the moon.'
segments.s084-g67: ازین در is an idiomatic locution meaning 'from this door/point' (از + این + در), here 'from this matter/point'. سخن 'speech' is the object of نراند 'drove away'; نراند is the negative past form 'did not drive away' (راندن in past with negative prefix ن-), and هیچ‌گونه 'no kind whatsoever' negates the object.
segments.s084-g68: به آرام = 'in calm/quietly' (به used adverbially + آرام). بر governs نامه (here 'record/book/document'), so بر نامه کین = 'the book (record) of vengeance/hatred'. نخواند is the negative past of خواندن 'did not read/recite' (here 'did not recite/read aloud'), so the clause means 'he did not, quietly, recite/read the book of vengeance.'
segments.s084-g69: اگر introduces a conditional clause. ما is the subject 'we'. نشوریم is a verbal form from 'نشور' (here 'to rise/rebel/stir'); in this conditional it expresses 'if we rise/if we make an upheaval' (the construction is a customary conditional: 'اگر ... بهتر بود' = 'it would be better if ...'). بهتر بود is the result clause 'it would have been better' (بود as past/counterfactual auxiliary).
segments.s084-g70: کزین = ک + این, 'from this' or 'because of this'. جنبش آشوب is a nominal cluster: جنبش 'movement' with آشوب 'turmoil' specifying its character. کشور بود is the predicate 'would be to the country' — idiomatically 'would bring turmoil upon the realm.' The final بود again marks the resulting state (past/conditional sense: 'would be').
segments.s084-g71: This is a fronted object/indirect-object construction: پسر را ('to the son' / 'the son' with definite object marker را), چنین 'thus', داد 'gave' (past of دادن), پاسخ 'answer' (the thing given), and پشنگ is the subject 'Pashang'. Word order is emphatic/classical: 'To the son thus gave answer Pashang.'
segments.s084-g72: که introduces the content of the answer: an equational/nominal clause with the copula omitted (typical in Persian). افراسیاب is the name, آن is the demonstrative 'that', and دلاور نهنگ is a compound nominal predicate: دلاور 'valiant/heroic' modifying نهنگ used metaphorically ('colossus/giant' rather than literal whale).
segments.s084-g81: This clause places the predicate phrase 'چراگاه اسپان شود' (literally 'becomes pasture of the steeds') before its subject 'کوه و دشت' — a common poetic inversion. شود is the present/historical third-person singular of شدن ‘to become’ (here functioning as a copula). اسپان is a plural collective for riding horses/steeds and governs چراگاه (pasture) in a possessive sense (چراگاهِ اسپان).
segments.s084-g82: گیاها is a plural collective subject 'the plants/herbage'. ز here marks the agent/source ('by/from') and links یال یلان (literally 'the mane of the heroes/warriors') to the verb برگذشت. برگذشت is a past/simple perfect sense 'passed over' or 'swept past' (intransitive here, meaning the manes moved over/swept the herbage).
segments.s084-g83: سر به سر is an idiom meaning 'throughout' or 'completely' and modifies جهان; the repeated سر is part of that fixed phrase rather than separate anatomical 'head' senses. گردد is the present/subjunctive/poetic form of شدن 'to become' (here 'becomes/turns'). ز خوید uses the archaic verb خوید 'to drink' in a causal/agentive sense: 'becomes green because of (drinking)'.
segments.s084-g84: به هامون is a prepositional phrase 'onto/to the plain'. سراپرده is a tent/inner curtain (an inner enclosure) that is the thing to be drawn. باید is the impersonal/modal 'it is necessary / one must'. کشید is the infinitival/root form of کشیدن 'to draw/stretch' used here with باید — 'must draw/extend'. Note: the provided sense list for کشید is imperfect for this context; here it functions as 'to draw/extend (a curtain)'.
segments.s084-g85: سپه را uses the postposition را as the definite/direct-object marker (making the army the object/topic of the action). همه intensifies 'the whole/entire'. سوی آمل is the directional phrase 'toward Amol'. براند is the 3rd-person singular present/historical of راندن 'to drive' (here a narrative/historical present meaning 'he drove/sent').
segments.s084-g86: دلی شاد is an adverbial phrase 'with a glad/joyful heart' modifying the action. بر + سبزه و گل indicates movement 'over/upon the greens and the flowers' (بر as a motion preposition). The verb براند repeats to complete the parallelism: 'drove (them) over the green and the flowers.'
segments.s084-g87: This is a noun phrase listing regions 'دهستان و گرگان' followed by همه 'all' and the locative/under marker زیر + نعل 'under the horseshoe' — idiomatically 'trodden under hoofs'. The phrase sets the scene for the verbs that follow.
segments.s084-g88: بکوبید is a past/historical verb 'they struck/beat' (from کوبیدن) describing the horses' hooves hitting the ground. The following وز here functions as a fused 'و ز' = 'and from' (classical/poetic contraction) introducing the source 'خون' (from blood); because the provided sense-list for وز does not include this conjunctive use, no listed sense matches exactly. کنید is the verb 'make/do' (here 'they made'), so خون کنید آب لعل means 'they made the water (be) ruby from blood' — i.e., the water was stained red like a ruby.
segments.s084-g1: A temporal adverbial: 'پس آنگه' is a poetic double adverb meaning 'then/thereupon' (both words emphasize sequence). 'ز' is the classical preposition 'from/after', and the following nominal phrase 'مرگ منوچهر شاه' places the noun 'مرگ' (death) before its possessor/name 'منوچهر' with the appositive title 'شاه' after the name — typical Persian noun + possessor + title order.
segments.s084-g2: 'آگهی بشد' is a fixed poetic phrasing meaning 'it became known / news arose' (بشد = 'it happened/it became'). 'تا' here functions as a conjunction introducing the reported content ('that...'), and the phrase 'به توران سپاه' is a deliberately inverted order (poetic) meaning 'an army to Turan' — i.e. 'that an army had gone/been sent to Turan' (the motion verb is implicit; direction is marked by 'به').
segments.s084-g3: 'ز' introduces a causal phrase here ('because of'). 'نارفتن' is a negative infinitive/gerund ('not going' / 'refusal to go') used as a noun, followed by 'کار نوذر' meaning 'Nowzar's affair/duty' (possessor after noun). 'همان' is an emphatic/connecting particle here ('that/thereupon') linking this cause to the reaction in the next segment.
segments.s084-g4: 'یکایک' is an adverb 'one by one / each in turn'. 'بگفتند' is past plural 'they said' (classical/preverbal b- prefix). The prepositional phrase 'با بدگمان' ('with suspicion' / 'suspiciously') modifies the verb; the direct object (what they said) is omitted and understood from context.
segments.s084-g5: 'چو' is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. 'بشنید' is the simple past 'he heard'; in poetic Persian the verb often precedes the subject, so the subject phrase 'سالار ترکان پشنگ' ('Pashang, commander of the Turks') follows the verb. 'سالار' is a title 'commander', 'ترکان' identifies the group 'the Turks', and 'پشنگ' is the proper name.
segments.s084-g6: 'چنان' ('thus/so') modifies the following verb. 'خواست' is the past tense 'he wished/desired'. 'کاید' here functions as the action intended ('to plot/contrive' — a verbal noun/verb sense in classical usage), so 'خواست کاید' = 'he resolved/desired to plot'. The phrase 'به ایران به جنگ' is again inverted: literally 'to Iran, to war' = 'to make war upon Iran' ('به' marks direction and 'جنگ' is 'war').
segments.s084-g7: 'یکی' 'someone/a certain one' is the subject. 'یاد کرد' (literally 'made remembrance') is a common poetic expression meaning 'mentioned' or 'recalled'. 'از' is the preposition 'of/regarding', and 'نیا زادشم' is a name/nominal phrase (here treated as 'ancestor Zādashm'), with 'نیا' ('ancestor') attached to the proper name.
segments.s084-g8: 'هم' ('also/likewise') links this line to the previous. 'از تور' ('from Tūr') gives origin; 'بر زد' is a poetic compound where 'زد' (literally 'struck') is used intransitively to mean 'sprang up/arose' or 'appeared' (common in epic diction). The following 'یکی تیز دم' is a nominal phrase describing the new figure: 'one [who is] sharp‑tailed' (an epithet — 'تیز' = sharp/quick, 'دم' here used figuratively as 'tail/point').
segments.s084-g33: اگر introduces a conditional protasis ('if'). زادشم is a proper name (the person/weapon owner). تیغ is the direct object 'sword' and برداشتی is the past-tense, second-person singular verb 'you took up/took' (classical narrative past). Together this segment gives the 'if' clause: 'If you took Zādashm's sword...'.
segments.s084-g34: جهان را uses را as the definite-object marker ('the world'). به marks the indirect relation/beneficiary 'to/for' (here 'to Garshasp'). نگذاشتی is the past negative 'you did not allow/you did not leave'; in this main clause it completes the conditional: 'you would not have left the world to Garshasp.'
segments.s084-g35: میان را ('the middle/middle course') takes را as the definite-object marker. ببستی is the past tense 'you closed/shut' (second/third-person past form used narratively). به + کین آوری expresses purpose/result: به + noun + verb phrase 'to bring (about) vengeance' (آوری = 'bring, cause'), so the clause means 'you shut the middle so as to bring vengeance.'
segments.s084-g36: بایران is an adjectival/noun-form meaning 'barren/fallow' (here part of what was or was not done). نکردی is the past negative 'you did not do.' مگر introduces an exception 'except/only,' and سروری is 'sovereignty/lordship.' The construction implies 'you did nothing except (seek) lordship.'
segments.s084-g37: کنون is the temporal adverb 'now.' هرچه 'whatever' + مانیده (past participle 'remaining') + بود (past 'was') form a noun phrase 'whatever had remained.' از marks origin/possessor 'from/of' and نیا means 'ancestor' — so 'whatever had remained from the ancestors.'
segments.s084-g38: ز (poetic variant of از) introduces the following predicate: کین جستن uses the noun کین 'vengeance' plus the infinitive جستن 'to seek' ('seeking vengeance'). و connects nouns in a list: چاره 'remedy/solution' and کیمیا 'kīmiyā' (here 'elixir' or secret remedy). Grammatically this phrase is the complement of 'بود' above: 'was seeking vengeance and (searching for) remedy and the elixir.'
segments.s084-g39: گشادنش is the verb گشادن 'to open' plus the enclitic object -ش 'it' ('open it'). بر + تیغ تیز expresses the instrument/means 'by/with the sharp sword' (تیز follows تیغ as adjective). منست is an archaic/classical copular/adjectival predicative meaning 'is fitting/appropriate.' The clause therefore reads 'Opening it with the sharp sword is fitting.'
segments.s084-g40: گه is an adverb 'occasionally/at times.' شورش و رستخیز are coordinated nouns 'turmoil/upheaval and uprising' (رستخیز here 'uprising'). منست repeats the copular/predicative 'is fitting,' producing a parallel clause: 'at times, turmoil and uprising are fitting.'
segments.s084-g25: کنون (=now) opens the clause; روز is the noun 'day/time' and the predicate follows. The poetic predicate tندی و کین جستنست uses two nouns joined by و and the archaic fused verb-ending -ست on the infinitive جستن (جستن + است → جستنست) to mean "is (a day) of seeking/seek." So the whole segment is a present-state nominal clause: "now is a day of fierceness and of seeking vengeance."
segments.s084-g26: رخ is the subject 'face/visage'; the following prepositional phrase از خون دیده ('from/of the eye's blood') modifies how the face is treated. گه is a noun 'occasion/time', and شستنست is the same archaic construction (شستن + است → شستنست) meaning 'is for washing' or 'it is time to wash.' The clause therefore says it is the time to wash the face with the eye's blood (i.e., to bathe the face in tears/blood).
segments.s084-g27: ز + گفت پدر is a poetic genitive/ablative: 'from/at the saying of the father.' مغز افراسیاب ('the marrow/brain of Afrasiab') is the theme being affected. Word order is topical (circumstance → verb → affected noun in the next segment), so this segment prepares: 'At his father's words, Afrasiab's mind...' (i.e. his inner core was stirred).
segments.s084-g28: برآمد is a past-tense verb 'arose/sprung up.' ز آرام 'from calm' indicates he was roused out of calmness. وز is the noun 'blast/gust'; خورد is the past of خوردن used intransitively as 'befell/struck' (here: 'the blast struck'), and خواب 'sleep' is its object. Together: 'he sprang from calm, and a blast struck (his) sleep' — a poetic way to say sleep was broken and he was roused.
segments.s084-g29: به پیش پدر is the locative phrase 'in the presence of/ before his father.' شد is the pastcopula/verb 'became,' and گشاده‌زبان is an adjective 'outspoken' used predicatively: 'he became outspoken before his father.' Note the adjective follows شد (became + adjective), a common Persian structure.
segments.s084-g30: دل آگنده از کین is a compact noun phrase: دل 'heart' + آگنده 'filled' + از کین 'with hatred/vengeance.' The final short phrase کمر برمیان is elliptical and idiomatic: کمر (here the girding/loins) + برمیان ('in the midst' / 'about him') conveys that he 'girded his loins' or prepared his strength for action. The two halves together describe his inner state and external readiness.
segments.s084-g31: که here introduces the content of his boast ('that...'). شایسته governs the following noun phrase directly (poetic omission of ezāfe): شایسته جنگ شیران means 'worthy/fit for the battle of lions' (شیران can be literal 'lions' or figurative 'brave warriors'). The clause ends with منم, the emphatic 'I am.'
segments.s084-g32: هم‌آورد is a noun 'equal'/'rival' placed before سالار ایران (the leader/chief of Iran); the final منم again identifies the speaker: 'I am the equal of the commander of Iran.' Word order is noun + title + copula, a terse boasty construction.

sentences.s084-s49: Thus spoke the famed Namjuy: “I will fill the stream with blood in vengeance.”
sentences.s084-s37: One is a male lion on the day of the hunt; another is a war‑elephant at the time of battle.
sentences.s084-s38: You too must be with him — to give a fortunate opinion in every greater and lesser matter.
sentences.s084-s39: A scion who sought vengeance on his ancestor — it is fitting that you should not call his lineage 'correct.'
sentences.s084-s40: When from the hem of the cloud's folds there is little left, the plain becomes filled with moisture from the rain.
sentences.s084-s5: About Manuchehr's deeds and his army, about the regiments and commanders, and about his country.
sentences.s084-s6: He summoned all the renowned men of his realm and the great men of his army.
sentences.s084-s7: Like Arjasp and Garsivaz and Barman; like Kalbad, the warlike Hezbar‑daman.
sentences.s084-s8: His spahbed, like Viseh Tizchang, who was commander over Pashang's host.
sentences.s084-s25: When the spahbed sees a worthy son, he brings the fitting one up to the sun's head.
sentences.s084-s26: After his death his post should be filled; he named Azira the son as guide.
sentences.s084-s27: When the war preparations were made, Aghrirath the guide came to the palace.
sentences.s084-s28: He stood before his father with a pensive heart, for his heart still bore a thought/concern.
sentences.s084-s45: Manuchehr, that warrior from that place, turned toward Tūr with hatred.
sentences.s084-s46: He strove in battle with Qāren; Garshasp, meanwhile, again moved about that assembly.
sentences.s084-s47: Could they (perhaps) reach the plain of vengeance against these two proud ones of the land of Iran?
sentences.s084-s48: Make the souls of our ancestors glad; set the hearts of the ill-omened aflame.
sentences.s084-s9: Afrasiab summoned his world-champion Poursh; after a short pause he came in haste.
sentences.s084-s10: He spoke of Tūr and of Salm, saying that such vengeance should not be hidden under the hem.
sentences.s084-s11: Is there anyone whose brain is not boiling? One cannot hide such a deed.
sentences.s084-s12: That is what the Iranians did to us: they perpetrated evils one by one.
sentences.s084-s29: Thus he said, 'O you whose father is experienced in affairs, a man has risen among the Turks.'
sentences.s084-s30: If Manuchehr's numbers in Iran have dwindled, the commander has become weak like Sam.
sentences.s084-s31: Except for such fighters as Garshasp and Qaren, that company has no other famed men.
sentences.s084-s32: Do you know what befell mighty Salm and Tur at the hands of that sword-wielding old wolf?
sentences.s084-s21: A rush of haste entered Pashang's mind when he saw Afrasiab's dark, towering figure.
sentences.s084-s22: He had the arm of a lion and the might of an elephant, and from him a shadow spread over several miles.
sentences.s084-s23: His tongue, in manner, was a cutting sword; his heart was like the sea, and his hand like a shower of blades.
sentences.s084-s24: He commanded that the sword of war be drawn and that (they) proceed to Iran with Pashang's army.
sentences.s084-s33: Of ancestral birth was the king of Turan's host, whose banner ever spreads over heaven and moon.
sentences.s084-s34: From this gate/point he drove away no sort of speech; he did not calmly read (recite) the book of vengeance.
sentences.s084-s35: It would have been better if we did not rise, for from this movement would come the country's turmoil.
sentences.s084-s36: Pashang thus gave answer to the son: 'That Afrasiab — that valiant colossus (whale).'
sentences.s084-s41: The mountains and plains became pastures for the steeds, and the herbage was swept aside by the manes of the heroes.
sentences.s084-s42: The whole world turns green everywhere from drinking (the moisture); one must draw the inner curtain onto the plain.
sentences.s084-s43: He drove the whole army toward Amol; with a glad heart he drove them over the greens and flowers.
sentences.s084-s44: Dehestān and Gorgan were all under the horseshoes; they beat (the ground), and with blood they made the water like a ruby.
sentences.s084-s1: Then, upon the death of King Manuchehr, it became known that an army had gone to Turan.
sentences.s084-s2: Because Nowzar would not go on the campaign, each one spoke with suspicion.
sentences.s084-s3: When Pashang, commander of the Turks, heard (this), he resolved thus to bring war upon Iran.
sentences.s084-s4: One mentioned his ancestor Zādashm; likewise, from Tūr there arose one, sharp‑tailed.
sentences.s084-s17: If you had taken Zādashm's sword, you would not have left the world to Garshasp.
sentences.s084-s18: You closed off the middle course to bring about enmity; you did nothing except (pursue) lordship.
sentences.s084-s19: Now whatever of the ancestral legacy had remained was devoted to seeking vengeance, remedies, and the elixir.
sentences.s084-s20: It is fitting to open it with my keen sword; at times turmoil and uprising are fitting.
sentences.s084-s13: Now is a day of fierceness and of seeking vengeance; it is time to wash the face with the eye's blood.
sentences.s084-s14: At his father's words Afrasiab's mind sprang from calmness; a blast struck (and drove away) his sleep.
sentences.s084-s15: Before his father he became outspoken; his heart, filled with hatred, girded his loins (prepared for action).
sentences.s084-s16: For I am fit for the battle of lions; I am the equal (rival) of the commander of Iran.
